questions.question: The Philippine-American War (1899-1902) followed the Spanish-American War. What was its cause?
options: The Philippines declared war on the US after a trade dispute over sugar
After the US purchased the Philippines from Spain, Filipino revolutionaries under Emilio Aguinaldo — who had expected independence — fought the US colonial administration instead
Spain secretly encouraged the Philippines to fight the US after the 1898 treaty
The US accidentally bombed Manila, triggering a war the US hadn't intended
questions.answer: After the US purchased the Philippines from Spain, Filipino revolutionaries under Emilio Aguinaldo — who had expected independence — fought the US colonial administration instead
questions.explanation: Emilio Aguinaldo's Philippine Revolutionary Army had fought alongside the US against Spain, expecting independence. Instead, the US paid Spain $20 million for the Philippines and declared it an American territory. The resulting Philippine-American War (1899-1902) killed approximately 200,000-600,000 Filipinos (including civilian deaths from famine and disease), 4,234 Americans, and 16,000 Filipino fighters. The war was marked by atrocities on both sides. The Philippines didn't achieve full independence until 1946.
questions.memory_hook: The Philippines fought the US for independence immediately after helping the US fight Spain — one colonial power replaced by another. The resulting war killed up to 600,000 Filipinos and is largely forgotten in American history despite being a significant counterinsurgency.

questions.question: The Boxer Rebellion (1900) in China targeted which groups?
options: Foreign merchants and Confucian scholars opposed to modernization
Missionaries and foreign nationals in China, as well as Chinese Christians — the 'Boxers' (Yihetuan movement) believed they were immune to bullets and sought to expel foreign influence
The Qing dynasty's Manchu rulers on behalf of Han Chinese nationalists
Japanese merchants and diplomats following Japan's victory in the 1895 Sino-Japanese War
questions.answer: Missionaries and foreign nationals in China, as well as Chinese Christians — the 'Boxers' (Yihetuan movement) believed they were immune to bullets and sought to expel foreign influence
questions.explanation: The Boxers (Yihetuan — 'Righteous Harmony Society') were a Chinese nationalist movement that practiced martial arts and believed ritual made them immune to foreign bullets. They targeted Christian missionaries, Chinese converts, and foreign nationals. The Qing Empress Dowager Cixi initially supported them. An international coalition (Britain, USA, France, Russia, Germany, Japan, Austria-Hungary, Italy) invaded China to relieve the Peking Legation Quarter siege. The Boxer Protocol (1901) imposed massive reparations on China. The rebellion deepened the Century of Humiliation.
questions.memory_hook: The Boxers believed ritual martial arts made them bulletproof — and convinced the Empress Dowager to declare war on eight countries simultaneously. The result: China was forced to pay reparations it took decades to clear. This is why the Boxer Protocol payments funded Chinese students to study in the US.

questions.question: Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolent resistance (Satyagraha) was used in the Indian independence movement. What was the 'Salt March' of 1930?
options: A protest against the British salt monopoly in which Gandhi and thousands of followers walked 240 miles to the sea to make salt illegally — defying British law and triggering mass civil disobedience
A peaceful parade celebrating Indian cultural heritage that was violently suppressed by British forces
A hunger strike Gandhi undertook to protest the arrest of independence leaders
A boycott of all British goods, symbolized by salt as a basic necessity
questions.answer: A protest against the British salt monopoly in which Gandhi and thousands of followers walked 240 miles to the sea to make salt illegally — defying British law and triggering mass civil disobedience
questions.explanation: Gandhi's 240-mile (386 km) Salt March (March-April 1930) from Ahmedabad to Dandi on the Arabian Sea coast was a protest against British salt taxes — which made Indians pay for a basic necessity controlled by colonial law. Gandhi and followers collected seawater and made salt illegally. The protest generated massive international press coverage and triggered nationwide civil disobedience. British forces arrested 60,000 people. The march is considered one of history's most effective acts of nonviolent resistance.
questions.memory_hook: Gandhi chose salt brilliantly — a basic necessity that every Indian needed, taxed and monopolized by colonizers. The 240-mile walk gave the press weeks of coverage before the 'illegal' salt-making even happened. By the time Gandhi picked up sea salt at Dandi, the whole world was watching.

questions.question: Which cyclist holds the most Tour de France titles, and over what period did they win?
questions.explanation: Lance Armstrong won 7 consecutive Tour de France titles (1999-2005) — all were stripped by USADA in 2012 for systematic doping. In Armstrong's era, the sport was thoroughly doped; almost every podium finisher in those years was later found to have doped. The next-highest titleholder whose titles stand is Eddy Merckx (5 titles: 1969-1974). Armstrong's USADA investigation documented one of sport's most sophisticated doping programs. He later admitted doping in a 2013 Oprah interview.
questions.memory_hook: Armstrong's 7 Tour titles were all stripped — the most dramatic fall in sports history. The investigation found systematic blood doping, EPO, and testosterone. Armstrong's teammate Tyler Hamilton later said doping in the peloton was 'like putting on a seatbelt' — universal and expected.

questions.question: The 'Thrilla in Manila' (1975) was one of boxing's most brutal fights. Who were the two combatants?
options: Sugar Ray Leonard and Roberto Duran
questions.explanation: The Thrilla in Manila (October 1, 1975, Araneta Coliseum, Philippines) was the third and final Ali-Frazier fight. Ali won when Frazier's trainer Eddie Futch stopped the fight before the 15th round — Frazier was partially blinded and nearly unconscious. Ali later said it was the closest thing to death he'd experienced. Both men suffered lasting health consequences. The three Ali-Frazier fights (Fight of the Century 1971, Super Fight II 1974, Thrilla in Manila 1975) are considered boxing's greatest trilogy.
questions.memory_hook: Ali said the Thrilla in Manila was 'the closest thing to dying' he'd experienced. Frazier, who was stopped with one round left, never forgave Ali for the racial taunts ('gorilla') before the fight. Their rivalry was personal in a way that transcended sport.

questions.question: 'The Silence of the Lambs' (1991) won all five major Academy Awards (Picture, Director, Actor, Actress, Screenplay). Who plays Hannibal Lecter?
questions.explanation: Anthony Hopkins won Best Actor for The Silence of the Lambs despite appearing on screen for only 16 minutes — one of the shortest performances to win Best Actor. Hopkins played Dr. Hannibal Lecter, a brilliant psychiatrist and cannibal who helps FBI agent Clarice Starling (Jodie Foster, Best Actress) catch a serial killer. The film is one of only three to win all five major Oscars (the others: It Happened One Night, 1934; One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest, 1975). Thomas Harris's novel was the source material.
questions.memory_hook: Hopkins won Best Actor for 16 minutes of screen time — the shortest winning performance in Oscar history. Hannibal Lecter became one of cinema's greatest villains from a film where he's almost a supporting character. The Silence of the Lambs is 1 of only 3 films to sweep all 5 major Oscars.

questions.question: Japanese anime director Hayao Miyazaki co-founded Studio Ghibli. Besides 'Spirited Away,' which of his films features a young witch who loses her magical powers?
options: Nausicaa of the Valley of the Wind
questions.explanation: Kiki's Delivery Service (1989) follows 13-year-old Kiki, a witch-in-training who moves to a new city to complete her year of independence — a traditional rite for young witches. She uses her only power (flying her broomstick) to start a delivery service but gradually loses her powers due to self-doubt and depression. The film is about creative block and finding your confidence. Miyazaki's films consistently feature strong, young female protagonists navigating adult challenges — Nausicaa, Kiki, Chihiro (Spirited Away), Sophie (Howl's Moving Castle), San (Princess Mononoke).
questions.memory_hook: Kiki's loss of her witch powers is Miyazaki's metaphor for creative block and depression — she doesn't lose them through defeat but through losing confidence in herself. The cure isn't practice or magic; it's reconnecting with why she loved flying in the first place.

questions.question: Which American musician recorded 'What's Going On' (1971), considered one of the greatest albums in soul and R&B history?
questions.explanation: Marvin Gaye's What's Going On (1971) addressed Vietnam, racial inequality, poverty, and environmental destruction — highly unusual topics for a Motown artist, which is why Motown initially refused to release it. After release, it became a massive commercial and critical success, with the title track, 'Mercy Mercy Me (The Ecology),' and 'Inner City Blues' all hitting the charts. Rolling Stone consistently ranks it as one of the greatest albums ever made. Gaye was shot and killed by his own father in 1984, the day before his 45th birthday.
questions.memory_hook: Motown refused to release What's Going On — Berry Gordy thought political music wouldn't sell. Marvin Gaye threatened to never record again. Gordy relented; the album became one of history's most acclaimed. Gaye was killed by his father in 1984, the day before his 45th birthday.

questions.question: The Volga River is Europe's longest river. Into which body of water does it flow?
questions.explanation: The Volga River (3,531 km) rises in the Valdai Hills northwest of Moscow and flows south and east into the Caspian Sea — an inland sea with no outlet to any ocean. The Volga drains about 40% of European Russia and has been a central artery of Russian history, culture, and commerce. The Battle of Stalingrad (1942-43) — one of WWII's most decisive — was fought along the Volga. About 11 major cities sit on the Volga, including Kazan and Volgograd (formerly Stalingrad). The phrase 'Mother Volga' reflects its cultural importance.
questions.memory_hook: The Volga empties into the Caspian Sea — an inland lake with no ocean connection — making it hydrologically isolated from global ocean circulation. The Battle of Stalingrad was fought for control of the Volga crossing; losing it would have cut off Soviet oil supplies from the Caspian region.

questions.question: The Gobi Desert spans parts of which two countries?
questions.explanation: The Gobi Desert covers approximately 1.3 million km² across southern Mongolia and northern China. It is Asia's largest desert and the world's fifth largest. Unlike the Sahara, the Gobi is a cold desert — temperatures range from -40°C in winter to +45°C in summer. It sits on a high plateau (~900-1,500m elevation) due to the Himalayas creating a rain shadow. The Gobi is expanding southward due to desertification driven by overgrazing, agriculture, and climate change — threatening Inner Mongolia and approaching Beijing with sand storms.
questions.memory_hook: The Gobi is a cold desert on a high plateau — temperatures swing 85°C between winter and summer. It's expanding toward Beijing: some years, dust storms from the Gobi deposit millions of tonnes of sediment on the Chinese capital. Beijing occasionally issues red-level dust alerts.

questions.question: What is the difference between a gene and an allele, and how does Mendelian genetics predict inheritance patterns?
options: Genes and alleles are synonyms; Mendelian genetics predicts that all traits are inherited equally from both parents

A gene is a specific location on a chromosome coding for a trait; an allele is a variant of that gene. Dominant alleles are expressed over recessive ones. Mendelian genetics predicts ratios of offspring traits using Punnett squares
Alleles are only found in plants; human genetics uses a different inheritance system
Dominant genes always eliminate recessive genes permanently from offspring
questions.answer: A gene is a specific location on a chromosome coding for a trait; an allele is a variant of that gene. Dominant alleles are expressed over recessive ones. Mendelian genetics predicts ratios of offspring traits using Punnett squares
questions.explanation: Gregor Mendel (1822-1884) crossed pea plants and discovered inheritance patterns. Each organism has two alleles for each gene (one from each parent). Dominant alleles (capital letters, e.g., B) are expressed when present; recessive (b) only when both copies are recessive (bb). A Punnett square crosses two parent genotypes to predict offspring probability. Example: two carriers (Bb × Bb) produce 25% BB (dominant), 50% Bb (carrier, dominant expression), 25% bb (recessive expression). This underlies genetic disease counseling.
questions.memory_hook: Mendel discovered genetics by counting peas in a monastery garden. He died without knowing about DNA — he just saw the ratios. The 3:1 ratio that appears in crosses between two carriers (like many recessive genetic diseases) is pure mathematics encoded in biology.

questions.question: What is the difference between potential and kinetic energy in electricity — specifically, what is voltage, current, and resistance?
options: Voltage is electricity speed; current is electricity volume; resistance is electricity color in conductors

Voltage (V) is the electrical potential difference (energy per charge); current (I, amperes) is the flow rate of charge; resistance (R, ohms) is opposition to flow. Ohm's Law: V = I × R. Together they describe how electricity behaves in circuits
Voltage and current are the same thing measured in different units
Resistance generates electricity; voltage and current are its byproducts
questions.answer: Voltage (V) is the electrical potential difference (energy per charge); current (I, amperes) is the flow rate of charge; resistance (R, ohms) is opposition to flow. Ohm's Law: V = I × R. Together they describe how electricity behaves in circuits
questions.explanation: Water analogy: voltage = water pressure; current = flow rate; resistance = pipe narrowness. Ohm's Law (V = IR): double the voltage at constant resistance, double the current. Power = V × I (watts). This is why high-voltage power lines (hundreds of kV) can transmit electricity long distances with low current — less energy lost to resistance heating. Your phone charger steps the voltage down from 120/240V to 5V. A short circuit removes resistance, allowing dangerous current surges.
questions.memory_hook: The water analogy: voltage = pressure, current = flow rate, resistance = pipe width. Ohm's Law connects them: V = IR. High-voltage power lines aren't dangerous because of volts alone — it's the combination of voltage and available current. Lightning is extremely high voltage but short duration; household current is lower voltage but continuous.
